ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated tartar sauce code; A0FKP (mayonnaise-type sauce) is the closest defensible analogous anchor, representing a parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for tartar sauce as a food item; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of tartar sauce is approximately 1 tablespoon (15 g), used as a dipping sauce or condiment alongside fried fish, seafood, or fish cakes.

nutrient_source.title: Tartar Sauce — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Tartar Sauce (cr_id F10050002). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for tartar sauce (FDC ID 172237) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat; all other values are from HPB.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate for mayonnaise-based condiment with low carbohydrate content
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tartar sauce in the University of Sydney GI Database. Tartar sauce contains only 4.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g, predominantly from pickle brine and minor starch. GI is estimated at approximately 25 (low) by analogy with other mayonnaise-based, low-carbohydrate condiments documented in Atkinson et al. (2021). Glycemic load per 15 g serving = (25 × 0.63 g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 0.2, which is negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content at 707 mg per 100 g
hypertension.guidance: Tartar sauce contains 707 mg sodium per 100 g, exceeding the 600 mg/100 g threshold; individuals with hypertension should limit use to no more than one teaspoon (5 g) per serving, contributing approximately 35 mg sodium, and should prefer homemade versions prepared with reduced-salt mayonnaise and lemon juice.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High caloric density from fat (531 kcal/100 g) with low carbohydrate content (4.2 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tartar sauce poses minimal direct glycaemic impact due to its low carbohydrate content (4.2 g/100 g) and estimated GI of 25, but its very high caloric density (531 kcal/100 g) from fat can contribute to weight gain and worsening insulin resistance if used liberally; individuals with type 2 diabetes should restrict portion size to one tablespoon (15 g) and check commercial labels for added sugars or sweet pickle relish.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~8.9 g/100 g), estimated trans fat (0.1 g/100 g), high sodium (707 mg/100 g), and cholesterol (51 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tartar sauce presents multiple cardiovascular risk factors including high saturated fat (~8.9 g/100 g), estimated trans fat (0.1 g/100 g from commercial mayonnaise bases), cholesterol (51 mg/100 g), and very high sodium (707 mg/100 g); individuals with cardiovascular disease should use it sparingly (≤15 g per occasion) and choose trans-fat-free, reduced-sodium homemade versions made with olive oil-based mayonnaise.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory saturated fat (~8.9 g/100 g) and estimated trans fat (0.1 g/100 g) combined with high sodium (707 mg/100 g)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Tartar sauce is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its pro-inflammatory saturated fat and estimated trans fat content from commercial mayonnaise bases, and its high sodium content which may exacerbate RA-related tissue swelling; homemade versions using extra-virgin olive oil-based mayonnaise and minimal salt are preferred.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tartar sauce is a cold, creamy condiment of Western European (primarily French) origin, widely used as a dipping sauce and accompaniment for fried fish, seafood, fish cakes, and chips. It is made by combining mayonnaise — an emulsion of egg yolk, oil, and acid — with finely chopped pickles (gherkins), capers, lemon juice, and sometimes onion, parsley, or mustard. The result is a thick, tangy, slightly briny sauce with a rich, fatty mouthfeel and a mild acidic brightness from the pickles and lemon. Tartar sauce is a processed condiment with a very high fat content (58.6 g/100 g), predominantly from the mayonnaise base, and a very high sodium content (707 mg/100 g), making it a high-concern food for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Its carbohydrate content is low (4.2 g/100 g), so it poses minimal direct glycaemic impact, but its caloric density (531 kcal/100 g) means liberal use can contribute to weight gain. In Singapore and Malaysia, tartar sauce is commonly served alongside Western-style fast food, fish and chips, and fried seafood dishes. Commercial versions may contain added sugars, preservatives, and higher trans fat levels than homemade preparations. A standard serving is approximately one tablespoon (15 g), contributing roughly 80 kcal, 8.8 g fat, and 106 mg sodium.
common_user_queries: What is tartar sauce made of?
How much sodium is in tartar sauce?
Is tartar sauce high in fat?
What is the difference between tartar sauce and mayonnaise?
Is tartar sauce suitable for hypertension?
How many calories in tartar sauce?
What do you eat tartar sauce with?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F10050002, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central tartar sauce entry as a secondary approved source. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, which is the only approved GI source and is clearly documented.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for tartar sauce is not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or confirmed in USDA FoodData Central at the level of individual unsaturated fatty acids for this composite condiment. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient data beyond potassium, calcium, iron, and cholesterol not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry; USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 172237) was consulted but values were not consistently available for all fields. trans_fat_g set to 0.1 based on USDA FoodData Central estimate for commercial tartar sauce; this is an approximation and may vary by brand. saturated_fat_g of 8.9 g/100 g estimated from USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 172237) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for tartar sauce exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Tartar sauce is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; it is a Western condiment of French origin, so no mandatory non-English local-language names are required under G6 multilingual rules.
